titlestmt.titleproper: Finding Aid for the Matsumi Kanemitsu papers LSC.2470
titlestmt.author: Finding aid prepared by Doug Johnson, 2025.

p.date: Online finding aid last updated 2026 February 24 by Caroline Cubé.

address.addressline: Room A1713, Charles E. Young Research Library

publicationstmt.p: Finding aid description and metadata in this record are licensed under a Creative Commons Public Domain Designation (CC0 1.0 Universal). See LSC's Metadata Rights Statement for more information.

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2026-02-24 10:42:24 -0800.

physdesc.extent: (79 boxes, 2 half-boxes, 9 flat boxes, 5 shoe boxes, 2 oversize boxes, 1 square box)

did.abstract:
Matsumi "Mike" Kanemitsu was born in 1922 in Ogden, Utah. He was sent to Japan at three years old to be raised by his grandparents on their family land outside of Hiroshima. By the time he was eighteen, he had moved back and forth between Japan and the United States several times. In the 1950s, he relocated to New York to study with Yasuo Kuniyoshi and attend the Art Students League on the GI Bill. His earliest work in drawing and painting was figurative. Moving away from Kuniyoshi's influence, he became one of the younger members of the New York School of Abstract Expressionists. Among his circle of friends were Franz Kline, Paul Jenkins, and Jackson Pollock, who gave him the nickname "Mike." One of his studios was in the same building as Robert Rauschenberg and Jasper Johns. In 1961, he received a Ford Foundation Fellowship to study at the Tamarind Lithography Workshop, which artist June Wayne founded in Hollywood just a year earlier. Though he identified as a painter throughout his career, this introduction to lithography had a profound impact on his life and art. He moved to Los Angeles permanently in the mid-1960s, and taught at the Chouinard Art Institute/California Institute of the Arts from 1965 to 1970 and at Otis College of Art and Design from 1971 to 1980. He maintained a thriving career as a painter until his death in 1992.
The collection contains records of dozens of group and solo exhibitions Kanemitsu participated in over the decades and posthumusly. It includes correspondence, exhibition flyers and catalogs, clippings, and photographs. There is also a great deal of maerial on other artists in the form of books, periodicals, exhibition catalogs and flyers, and resumes. There is some audiovisual material, mostly documenting various art events. There are also more than two dozen examples of Kanemitsu's original artwork.
did.physloc: Portions of the collection stored off-site. Advance notice is required for access to the collection. All requests to access special collections materials must be made in advance using the request button located on this page.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Open for research. All requests to access special collections materials must be made in advance using the request button located on this page.

phystech.head: Physical Characteristics and Technical Requirements
phystech.p: CONTAINS UNPROCESSED DIGITAL and AUDIOVISUAL MATERIALS: Digitaland audiovisual materials are not currently available for access and will require further processing and assessment. If you have questions about this material please email AskLSC@library.ucla.edu.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Reproduction and Use
userestrict.p: Property rights to the physical objects belong to the UCLA Library Special Collections. All other rights, including copyright, are retained by the creators and their heirs. It is the responsibility of the researcher to determine who holds the copyright and pursue the copyright owner or his or her heir for permission to publish where The UC Regents do not hold the copyright.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: [Identification of item], Matsumi Kanemitsu papers (LSC.2470). UCLA Library Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, University of California, Los Angeles.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived user interest and research value, availability of staff and resources, and competing priorities. Library Special Collections provides a standard level of preservation and access for all collections and, when time and resources permit, conducts more intensive processing. These materials have been arranged and described according to national and local standards and best practices.

We are committed to providing ethical, inclusive, and anti-racist description of the materials we steward, and to remediating existing description of our materials that contains language that may be offensive or cause harm. We invite you to submit feedback about how our collections are described, and how they could be described more accurately, by filling out the form located on our website: Report Problematic Content and Description in UCLA's Library Collections and Archives.

bioghist.head: Administrative/Biographical History
bioghist.p: Matsumi "Mike" Kanemitsu was born in 1922 in Ogden, Utah to Kichizaimon and Shizuma Kanemitsu, both issei, or first-generation Japanese immigrants. A kibei nisei, Kanemitsu was sent back to Japan at three years old to be raised by his grandparents on their family land outside of Hiroshima. By the time he was eighteen, he had moved back and forth between Japan and the United States several times. These early experiences connecting to nature in rural Japan, daily training in calligraphy and martial arts, as well as his idiosyncratic movement through places and cultures shaped some of the enduring elements of his artistic practice.

As a teenager, he became increasingly critical of Japan's rising militarism and left permanently in 1940. Shortly after his arrival in the United States he realized he would not be able to avoid the draft and volunteered, requesting a low-risk placement that would contribute to his development as an artist. When Pearl Harbor was attacked, he was interrogated and held with other prisoners of war. A military librarian, Virginia Hennessy Freal, befriended him and gave him art magazines and Red Cross art supplies. During the war, he traveled extensively throughout the United States and Europe, moving from different military bases as a prisoner and then a low-ranking soldier. Later, he visited as many museums as he could in France, England, and the Netherlands. He also made his first connections with professional artists, first in a small scene in Colorado, and then Paris and the south of France, where he met artists including Henry Moore, Henri Matisse, Pablo Picasso, and Yasuo Kuniyoshi.

After he was discharged in 1946, Kanemitsu remained in France to study with Fernand Léger on the GI Bill. That year he also married his first wife, Mildred Hunt, and in 1948 they returned to the United States to live in her hometown of Baltimore, Maryland. His early supporters in Baltimore included the artist Karl Metzler and collector J. Blankfard Martenet. In the 1950s, he relocated to New York to study with Kuniyoshi and attend the Art Students League, also on the GI Bill. His earliest work in drawing and painting was figurative. Moving away from Kuniyoshi's influence, he became one of the younger members of the New York School of Abstract Expressionists. Among his circle of friends were Franz Kline, Paul Jenkins, and Jackson Pollock, who gave him the nickname "Mike." One of his studios was in the same building as Robert Rauschenberg and Jasper Johns. As would be the case throughout his life, his enthusiastic involvement in various artistic circles contributed to the development of his work. His teachers and mentors were also his friends and colleagues, and later he would develop similar relationships with his students and younger artists. He was married to his second wife Lenore Pettit from 1956 until her death in 1959.

In the late 1950s and early 1960s, in addition to showing at Stephen Radich Gallery in New York, he had solo exhibitions every year with Virginia Dwan Gallery in Los Angeles. He was also included in group exhibitions at the Museum of Modern Art and the Whitney Museum of American Art. In 1961, he received a Ford Foundation Fellowship to study at the Tamarind Lithography Workshop, which artist June Wayne founded in Hollywood just a year earlier. Though he identified as a painter throughout his career, this introduction to lithography had a profound impact on his life and art. In 1973, Tamarind produced a film Four Stones for Kanemitsu, capturing his innovative, intuitive approach to lithography, which blended the best of his diverse skills and sensibilities. In 1967, he spent several months at the Honolulu Academy of Arts, where he had a solo exhibition.

He moved to Los Angeles permanently in the mid-1960s, where he raised his three children Patia, Zoe, and Paul largely as a single father, after divorcing their mother and his third wife Carole Donovan in the 1970s. In Los Angeles, he taught at the Chouinard Art Institute/California Institute of the Arts from 1965 to 1970 and at Otis College of Art and Design from 1971 to 1980. He also held temporary teaching positions at UC Berkeley and UC Santa Barbara. While at Otis, he met Nancy Uyemura, a young artist, Los Angeles native, and sansei (third generation) who would facilitate his connection to the city's Japanese American community. In the mid-1980s, they moved into a former warehouse at 800 Traction Avenue in the Downtown Los Angeles neighborhood that became designated as the Arts District in the 1990s. Kanemitsu's diverse networks in Japan, Europe, and the United States greatly contributed to the cultural life at 800 Traction and the surrounding neighborhood. With two Japanese gallerists, Kanemitsu and Uyemura opened Gallery IV at 800 Traction in 1990. Kanemitsu was a point of connection for artists coming from Japan to Los Angeles and the United States. He was involved with initiatives such as Friends of Little Tokyo Arts and had solo exhibitions at the Los Angeles Municipal Art Gallery (1978), the Japanese American Cultural and Community Center (1988), and The National Museum of Art, Osaka and Hiroshima Museum of Contemporary Art (1998).
In May 1992, Kanemitsu died of lung cancer at home, just a few weeks before his 70th birthday.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Content
scopecontent.p: The collection contains records of dozens of group and solo exhibitions Kanemitsu participated in over the decades and posthumusly. It includes correspondence, exhibition flyers and catalogs, clippings, and photographs. There is also a great deal of maerial on other artists in the form of books, periodicals, exhibition catalogs and flyers, and resumes. There is some audiovisual material, mostly documenting various art events. There are also more than two dozen examples of Kanemitsu's original artwork.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p:
This collection has been arranged in the following series:
Series 1: Personal/biographical material, 1950-2019
Subseries 1.1: Correspondence, 1956-1999
Subseries 1.2: Personal materials, 1952-2006
Subseries 1.3: Clippings and publicity, 1961-2008
Subseries 1.4: Memorial, 1992-1993
Series 2: Business, 1950-2019
Series 3: Photographs
Subseries 3.1: Photographs of people and events
Subseries 3.2: Photographs of artwork
Series 4: Books, periodicals, and catalogs
Series 5: Artworks and art supplies
Series 6: Artists' files
Series 7: Audiovisual material

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a letter from Virginia Freal reminiscing about knowing Kanemitsu and other Japanese people at Fort Riley, Kansas during World War II.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes photographs of the portrait Twitchell made of Kanemitsu.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes: note from Jerry Brown; two handwritten letters from Jules Horelick; three letters from Paul Jenkins [one handwritten]; note from Patia Kanemitsu; three letters from Sara Kuniyoshi [with a copy of Kanemitsu's reply]; handwritten note from Yayoi Kusama [photocopy]; letter from June Wayne.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes letters from: Paul Jenkins; Patia Kanemitsu; Yoji Kanemitsu [in Japanese]; Yayoi Kusama [in Japanese]; June Wayne.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes two letters from Paul Jenkins.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes: several letters from Toshiaki Izumi [folders 24-29]; letter from Yayoi Kusama [folder 57].

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes form letter, signed by Lloyd Goodrich and Robert Rauschenberg, asking Kanemitsu to donate an artwork to Artists for SEDF [Scholarship, Education and Defense Fund for Racial Equality].

did.unittitle: Letter to Yoji and Keiko Kanemitsu [brother and sister-in-law]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A long, handwritten letter, with English translation.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondents include: Toshiaki Izumi, Shigenobu Kimura, Sara Mazo Kuniyoshi [in English], Yayoi Kusama [in English], Yasuhito Matsuo, Michio Teramoto.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes articles from the Tozai Times and New York Yomiuri.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also includes a book about cockatiels.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes guides to: the Old North Church; Kagoshima; Kamakura [in Japanese].

did.unittitle: New York School artists and bibliography
Interview with Nancy Uyemara by Paul J. Karlstrom
Gutai: On-the-Spot Report by Jules Langsner
Manifesto on Transparent Sculpture by Roman A. Clef

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence regarding a racist review by Kristine McKenna.

phystech.head: Physical Characteristics and Technical Requirements
phystech.p: CONTAINS UNPROCESSED DIGITAL MATERIALS: digital materials are not currently available for access and will require further processing and assessment. If you have questions about this material please email AskLSC@library.ucla.edu.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: List of press outlets with notes describing interactions with them. Includes CD-R.

did.unittitle: Kanemitsu mentioned in books and periodicals

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes: Transpacific, vol. 7, no. 4; Visions Art Quarterly, 1993 Spring; Kids Explore America's Japanese American Heritage.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes; The International Art vol. II no. II [in Japanese]; The Japan Times Weekly, 1964 May 23; Artweek, 1978 November 11 and 1985 January 5; Neworld 1979 November/December; Artfax, 1989 Autumn; Japanese American National Museum Bulletin, 1989/1990 Winter; Art-Talk, 1990 February; The Saxon-Lee Art Report, 1990 February 1; ArtScene 1993 January; "Works of Art without Names of Artists" by Tanida Etsuji.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Posters for exhibits at Japanese American Comunity Cultural Center and Hiroshima City Museum of Contemporary Art.

did.unittitle: Oversize newspapers with obituaries of Kanemitsu

odd.head: General
odd.p: These were possibly given out at the memorial services.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains records of Kanemitsu's shows in a variety of museums and galleries, both during his lifetime and posthumously, as well as material related to his teaching career. It also contains records of shows at Gallery IV. Materials consist of correspondence, catalogs, flyers, inventories, sales records, and books.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Material on an exhibition at the Pollock-Krasner House.

did.unittitle: Los Angeles County Museum of Art

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes research material by Ayako Yoshida.

phystech.head: Physical Characteristics and Technical Requirements
phystech.p: CONTAINS UNPROCESSED AUDIO MATERIALS: Audio materials are not currently available for access and will require further processing and assessment. If you have questions about this material please email AskLSC@library.ucla.edu.

International Council of MOMA - Fourteen Americans
Hiroshima City Museum of Contemporary Art
Pacific Standard Time / Norton Simon Museum

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes many photographs of Kanemitsu's work.

scopecontent.p: Includes photographs of works by Gary Lang.

did.unittitle: Homage to Kanemitsu by Nancy Uyemura

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes: flyer for the Dwan Gallery that has been painted on, 1959; magazine Pictures on Exhibit, 1959 June; flyer for Quarter to Five, an unproduced film about Kanemitsu, circa 2004.

did.unittitle: Clippings and exhibit flyers and catalogs

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes The Arts Review, vo. XV, no. 11. Also includes statements by Kanemitsu and a small amount of correspondence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes: 1973 calendars illustrated with Kanemitsu's paintings; flyers and catalogs; correspondence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes: exhibit flyers; correspondence; The Japanese American Library Bulletin, 1990 Winter.

A Guide to the Collection: Hiroshima Prefectural Art Museum

scopecontent.p: Includes job offers from universities, as well as a record of what Kanemitsu earned throughout the 1960s.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes information on a 1969 lawsuit filed by several faculty and students.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Material on an exhibit about the Art Institute.

June Wayne exhibit at Barnsdall Art Park

scopecontent.p: Most are unidentified, but includes: John Altoon; Norman Bluhm; William Brice; George Gurdjieff; Yayoi Kusama; Joe Slusky.

Negatives and transparencies of sumi paintings

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Although the photographs were taken between 1979 and 1990, some artworks date from as early as 1956.

did.unittitle: Black and white prints of lithographs
Black and white prints of lithographs

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Though the photographs are undated the artworks depicted span the years 1949 to 1982.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains publications that are primarily about artists other than Kanemitsu.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this series are arranged very inconsistently. They are in alphabetical order, but sometimes the title of the exhibit is used, sometimes the venue, and sometimes even the publisher. We could have imposed consistency in the logic of alphabetization, but have chosen to maintain the donor's original arrangement.

did.unittitle: An Emotional Memoir of Franz Kline by Fielding Dawson
New York Contemporary Art 1960-1988 by Tatsuo Kondo

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Two books about Takeshi Kawashima, one a catalog of a show at the Takamatsu City Museum of Art.

scopecontent.p: Issue is about Kubo (Carlos Itsuo), a Japanese Brazilian srtist.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes handwritten note from the author.

did.unittitle: New York Contemporary Art, 1960-1988 by Tatsuo Kondo

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a piece of paper signed by several members of the group.

did.unittitle: Miyagi Museum of Art Permanent Exhibition Catalog
Osaka Prefecture Contemporary Art Collection Guide

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The June 10 issue includes an article by Kanemitsu: "My Greatest Benefactor's Suicide." The folder includes a 2023 English translation by Bradly Hammond.

did.physdesc: A series of 6x6 cards wih text, art, and photographs in a plastic case.

did.unittitle: Hiroshima, City Dedicated to the Cause of World Peace

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Newsletter of the Hiroshima Prefectural Art Museum.

Cunnilingus and Fellatio by John R. Trimble

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The box contains a large number of business cards.

did.unittitle: Drawing by Paul Jenkins and Kathy

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Fifty-five very small works on paper and one piece of molded plastic.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Twenty-four paintings in ink and watercolor.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Files typically include resumes and photographs of artworks.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes handmade holiday cards addresed to Mike and Nancy.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Exhibit catalog inscribed by the artist.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also includes catalog for Drew Klausner exhibit.

phystech.head: Physical Characteristics and Technical Requirements
phystech.p: CONTAINS UNPROCESSED AUDIOVISUAL MATERIALS: Audiovisual materials are not currently available for access and will require further processing and assessment. If you have questions about this material please email AskLSC@library.ucla.edu.

did.unittitle: The First Annual Exhibition of the World Artist Association of Beverly Hills
Matsumi Kanemitsu: A Japanese American Artist

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Three versions: no narration, English narration, and Japanese narration. VHS.
